—In this psalm we see David declaring how he intended to manage his household and govern his kingdom so that he could stop wickedness and encourage godliness. It is also applicable to private families, and it is the householder's psalm. It teaches all who have any power, whether more or less, to use it so as to be a terror to evil-doers and a praise to those who do well. The chosen subject of the psalm is God’s mercy and judgment. The Lord’s providences concerning his people are commonly mixed; mercy and judgment. God has set one over against the other, both to do good, like showers and sunshine. When, in his providence, he exercises us with a mixture of mercy and judgment, we must make suitable acknowledgments to him for both. Family mercies and family afflictions are both calls to family religion. Those who are in public stations are not excused from care in governing their families; they are even more concerned to set a good example of ruling their own houses well. Whenever a man has a house of his own, let him seek to have God dwell with him; and those may expect his presence who walk with a perfect heart, in a perfect way. David resolves to practice no evil himself. He further resolves not to keep bad servants, nor to employ those about him that are wicked. He will not admit them into his family, lest they spread the infection of sin. A stubborn heart, one that delights to be cross and perverse, is not fit for society, the bond of which is Christian love. Nor will he countenance slanderers, those who take pleasure in wounding their neighbor’s reputation. Also, God resists the proud and deceitful people who do not scruple to tell lies or commit frauds. Let every one be zealous and diligent to reform his own heart and ways, and to do this early; ever mindful of that future, most awful morning when the King of righteousness shall cut off all wicked doers from the heavenly Jerusalem.
